does: This paper introduces a holistic evaluation framework for AI agents that diagnoses failures across multi-step processes through a two-level approach: top-down agent-level diagnosis paired with bottom-up span-level evaluation. The method decomposes agent execution traces into independent per-span assessments, enabling precise localization of where and why failures occur rather than simply reporting success or failure outcomes. The framework is evaluated on TRAIL, GAIA, and SWE-Bench benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art results with substantial improvements: up to 38% relative gain on category F1 scores, 3.5x improvement in localization accuracy, and 12.5x improvement in joint localization-categorization accuracy. Notably, the same frontier model achieves several times higher localization accuracy when embedded in this framework compared to when used standalone.
connects: This work builds on prior efforts to move beyond coarse outcome-based evaluation of language models and agents toward more interpretable process-level assessment. It responds to limitations in existing agent evaluation methods that either provide only aggregate success metrics without diagnostic value or struggle to pinpoint failure sources in long execution traces. The approach extends research on structured trace analysis and compositional evaluation by introducing a scalable decomposition strategy that handles traces of arbitrary length. It connects to work on failure categorization, error analysis frameworks, and interpretability of multi-step reasoning, while drawing on advances in using frontier models for nuanced judgment tasks.
enables: This framework enables future work to build richer evaluation systems that not only measure agent performance but provide actionable diagnostic information for improvement. The span-level decomposition approach opens pathways for automated error classification at fine granularity, supporting more targeted debugging of agent systems and identification of systematic weaknesses. The demonstrated gains from structured prompting within frontier models suggest potential for developing more sophisticated agent evaluation pipelines that combine reasoning hierarchies with precise error localization. These capabilities could feed into automated agent improvement loops, enable comparative benchmarking of agent components, and support development of agents designed to be more interpretable by evaluation systems, ultimately advancing toward more transparent and debuggable autonomous systems.
